perturbation.original_text: Unless Shipper and Transporter have agreed to a rate other than the maximum rate, rates shall be Transporter's maximum rates and charges plus all applicable surcharges in effect from time to time under the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) on file with the Commission unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless Shipper and Transporter have agreed to a rate other than the market rate, rates shall be Transporter's maximum rates and charges plus all applicable surcharges in effect from time to time under the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) on file with the Commission unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'maximum rate' to 'market rate' creates ambiguity. The term 'maximum rate' in natural gas transportation is typically tied to tariffs filed with FERC, providing a clear legal standard. 'Market rate' is subjective and undefined in this context, potentially conflicting with FERC's regulatory authority over interstate gas transportation rates.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 717c - Rates and charges; schedules; suspension of new rates
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Natural Gas Act requires that all rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is hereby declared unlawful. The modified text uses 'market rate' instead of 'maximum rate' potentially allowing for rates that are not just and reasonable as reviewed and approved by the FERC.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 717c - Rates and charges | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 717c - Rates and charges U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Just and reasonable rates and charges All rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, and all rules and regulations affecting or pertaining to such rates or charges, shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful. (b) Undue preferences and unreasonable rates and charges prohibited No natural-gas company shall, with respect to any transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, (1) make or grant any undue preference or advantage to any person or subject any person to any undue prejudice or disadvantage, or (2) maintain any unreasonable difference in rates, charges, service, facilities, or in any other respect, either as between localities or as between classes of service. (c) Filing of rates and charges with Commission; public inspection of schedules Under such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe, every natural-gas company shall file with the Commission, within such time (not less than sixty days from June 21, 1938 ) and in such form as the Commission may designate, and shall keep open in convenient form and place for public inspection, schedules showing all rates and charges for any transportation or sale subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, and the classifications, practices, and regulations affecting such rates and charges, together with all contracts which in any manner affect or relate to such rates, charges, classifications, and services. (d) Changes in rates and charges; notice to Commission Unless the Commission o
perturbation.justification: The change from 'maximum rate' to 'market rate' contradicts the Natural Gas Act. 15 U.S. Code § 717c (a) states that 'All rates and charges...shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful.' By using 'market rate,' the modified text introduces ambiguity and potentially allows for rates that are not 'just and reasonable' as determined under the regulatory framework overseen by the Commission. This deviation from a clearly defined 'maximum rate' to a subjective 'market rate' directly undermines the legal requirement for just and reasonable rates as mandated by the Natural Gas Act.

perturbation.original_text: Transporter may file and seek Commission approval under Section 4 of the Natural Gas Act (NGA) at any time and from time to time to change any rates, charges or provisions set forth in the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) and the "General Terms and Conditions" in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, and Transporter shall have the right to place such changes in effect in accordance with the NGA, and this Agreement shall be deemed to include such changes and any such changes Source: TC PIPELINES LP, 10-K, 2/26/2016 which become effective by operation of law and Commission Order, without prejudice to Shipper's right to protest the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Transporter may file and seek Commission consultation under Section 4 of the Natural Gas Act (NGA) at any time and from time to time to change any rates, charges or provisions set forth in the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) and the "General Terms and Conditions" in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, and Transporter shall have the right to place such changes in effect in accordance with the NGA, and this Agreement shall be deemed to include such changes and any such changes Source: TC PIPELINES LP, 10-K, 2/26/2016 which become effective by operation of law and Commission Order, without prejudice to Shipper's right to protest the same.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'approval' with 'consultation' weakens the requirement for FERC's explicit authorization. The NGA requires Commission approval for rate changes, not just consultation. This misrepresents the regulatory process and could lead to disputes over the validity of unilaterally implemented rate changes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 717c - Rates and charges; schedules; suspension of new rates
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the Natural Gas Act mandates that rate changes must be approved by the FERC to be just and reasonable. Changing 'approval' to 'consultation' implies that the transporter only needs to discuss the changes with the FERC, not necessarily obtain their explicit consent, thus contradicting the legal requirement for approved rates.

(d) Changes in rates and charges; notice to Commission Unless the Commission o
perturbation.justification: The change from 'approval' to 'consultation' directly contradicts the requirement for FERC to approve rate changes under the Natural Gas Act, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both referencing 15 USC 717c, indicate that rates must be 'just and reasonable' and subject to the 'jurisdiction of the Commission.' By changing 'approval' to 'consultation,' the modified text circumvents the Commission's jurisdictional authority to approve rates, implying a lesser level of oversight. This contradicts the explicit requirements of 15 U.S.C. § 717c.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy between the parties arising under this Agreement and not resolved by the parties shall be determined in accordance with the laws of the State of Michigan.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy between the parties arising under this Agreement and not resolved by the parties shall be determined in accordance with principles of equity.
perturbation.explanation: Switching from 'laws of the State of Michigan' to 'principles of equity' removes a clear jurisdictional standard. While equity can be a consideration in legal disputes, relying solely on it disregards statutory and case law. This creates uncertainty and could contradict established legal precedents applicable to contract disputes within Michigan.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute establishes the jurisdiction of Michigan courts and the application of Michigan law within the state. Substituting 'principles of equity' for 'laws of the State of Michigan' attempts to bypass established statutory and case law, undermining the role of the Michigan legal system in resolving contractual disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 600.601 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 600.601 Download Section Chapter 600 Act 236 of 1961 236-1961-6 Previous Section Next Section REVISED JUDICATURE ACT OF 1961 (EXCERPT) Act 236 of 1961 600.601 Circuit court; jurisdiction and power. Sec. 601. (1) The circuit court has the power and jurisdiction that is any of the following: (a) Possessed by courts of record at the common law, as altered by the state constitution of 1963, the laws of this state, and the rules of the supreme court. (b) Possessed by courts and judges in chancery in England on March 1, 1847, as altered by the state constitution of 1963, the laws of this state, and the rules of the supreme court. (c) Prescribed by the rules of the supreme court. (2) The circuit court has exclusive jurisdiction over condemnation cases commenced under the drain code of 1956, 1956 PA 40, MCL 280.1 to 280.630. (3) In a judicial circuit in which the circuit court is affected by a plan of concurrent jurisdiction adopted under chapter 4, the circuit court has concurrent jurisdiction with the probate court or the district court, or both, as provided in the plan of concurrent jurisdiction. (4) The family division of circuit court has jurisdiction as provided in chapter 10. History: 1961, Act 236, Eff. Jan. 1, 1963 ;-- Am. 1996, Act 388 , Eff. Jan. 1, 1998 ;-- Am. 2002, Act 678 , Eff. Apr. 1, 2003 ;-- Am. 2005, Act 326 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 27, 2005 ;-- Am. 2012, Act 338 , Eff. Jan. 1, 2013
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies disputes are to be resolved in accordance with the laws of Michigan, establishing a clear legal standard. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity,' which undermines the application of Michigan statutory and case law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL 600.601, establishes the jurisdiction and power of the Michigan circuit court, derived from common law, the state constitution, state laws, and supreme court rules. Substituting 'principles of equity' directly contradicts the established legal framework as equity operates within that framework and is not a substitute for it. The modification removes the clear application of Michigan law, creating a significant contradiction because it bypasses the defined jurisdictional standard.
